description: Philological and literary study of the Hebrew text of Isiah 1-39. Readings in the ancient translations.
Philological and literary study of the Hebrew narratives in Genesis through Deuteronomy, with readings in the ancient translations.
Individual study for research and completing theses, as arranged with a faculty member.
Philological and literary study of the Hebrew text of Job. Readings in the ancient translations.
Critical reading of selected texts from the Latter Prophets and the Writings.

Philological and linguistic study of the Book of Psalms in Hebrew, with readings in the ancient translations. Special attention will be given to the book's bearing on our understanding of ancient Israelite religion(s).
Independent study as arranged with a faculty member.
Examine topics relevant to Biblical Studies through textual analysis and research.
A philological and comparative study, in Hebrew, of legal codes embedded in the narratives of Exodus, Leviticus, and Deuteronomy.

An intensive introduction to the grammar and vocabulary of Classical (Biblical) Hebrew, the language of the Hebrew Bible and Ancient Israel, equivalent toHEBR-BIB 103and104. Gain skills in preparation for reading the Hebrew Bible in the original language. Focus on translation of Hebrew text (written grammar and lexicon). Includes aspects of ancient Israelite culture, history, and literature.

optimized_prerequisites.prerequisites_text: Not open to students with credit forHEBR-BIB 103or104.

prerequisites.prerequisites_text: Not open to students with credit forHEBR-BIB 103or104.

description: An introduction to the thought, literature, and history of the prophets of ancient Israel (in English).
Readings in Peshitta, literary texts.

Provides graduate-level instruction in languages required for research in the literature, history, and culture of the Ancient Near East. Languages typically offered include Akkadian, Phoenician, Sumerian, Hittite, and Egyptian.
Philological and literary study of the Hebrew text of Isiah 40-66. Readings in the ancient translations.
Critical reading of selected texts from the Minor Prophets and the Writings.

course_title: CLASSICAL HEBREW LINGUISTICS:  HISTORICAL AND DESCRIPTIVE
description: The phonology, morphology and syntax of biblical Hebrew; viewed from historical and descriptive linguistic perspectives.
Forms and syntax, reading of Classical Hebrew prose in preparation for research.
Syriac grammar and reading.
Introduction to the language of the Hebrew Bible (Biblical Hebrew). Serves as basis for later forms of Classical Hebrew.
Review of grammar, introduction to reading narrative texts in Biblical Hebrew.
Grammar and readings in Biblical Aramaic (Daniel, Ezra).
Forms and syntax, reading of Classical Hebrew prose in preparation for research.
Review of grammar, introduction to reading narrative texts in Biblical Hebrew.
Selections from non-biblical Aramaic texts: Inscriptions, Elephentine letters, Ahiqar, Targumim.
Phoenician, Punic, Moabite, and Hebrew epigraphic texts.
Introduction to the language of the Hebrew Bible (Biblical Hebrew). Serves as basis for later forms of Classical Hebrew.
Readings in mythological, administrative, and epistolary texts from Ras Shamra-Ugarit.
